summary: A New York judge ruled that teacher Sheri Lederman's growth score evaluation was 'arbitrary and capricious,' validating long-standing criticisms of value-added models in education. Lederman's score dropped from 14 to 1 between years despite her students performing consistently well on state tests. The judge found multiple flaws in the statistical methodology, including unfair penalties for teachers with very high or low-performing students, unreliability with small class sizes, and the inability to explain year-to-year score swings. O'Neil sees this ruling as corroboration of her critiques of algorithmic evaluation systems.
key_points: Growth scores and value-added models can produce wildly inconsistent results for the same teacher with similar student outcomes
The statistical method unfairly penalizes teachers at both extremes—those with very high-performing or very low-performing students
Small class sizes make growth models less reliable, yet they're still used to evaluate teachers
The system defines a fixed percentage of teachers as 'ineffective' each year regardless of actual student performance trends
The state could not explain why Lederman's score swung so dramatically despite consistent student performance
money_quotes: I'm feeling extremely corroborated this week, what with the ProPublica report on Monday and also the recent judge's ruling on a teacher's growth score.
McDonough decided to vacate her score, describing it as 'arbitrary and capricious'.
He found that Lederman's small class size made the growth model less reliable.
He was troubled by the state's inability to explain the wide swing in Lederman's score from year to year, even though her students performed at similar levels.
He was perplexed that the growth model rules define a fixed percentage of teachers as ineffective each year, regardless of whether student performance across the state rose or fell.
key_insight: When algorithmic evaluation systems produce wildly inconsistent scores that the state cannot explain, and mandate fixed failure rates regardless of actual performance, they fail the basic test of fairness that any consequential scoring system should meet.
